text:
Dear Professor Lindqvist,

I've been meaning to write this for a while, and a quiet afternoon finally gave me the nudge. I wanted to thank you, properly and without the rush of a hallway goodbye, for everything you gave me over the years we worked together.

When I first walked into your office, I was capable enough on paper but genuinely unsure of myself underneath it. What I remember most is that you never handed me the answer. You'd lean back, ask one more question, and wait while I squirmed toward something better than what I'd come in with. At the time it was maddening. In hindsight it was the most generous thing you could have done, because it taught me to trust my own thinking instead of borrowing someone else's.

You also showed me a kind of patience I hadn't seen modeled before. The afternoon I brought you a draft I was embarrassed by, you read the whole thing carefully and then talked about its two good ideas before you mentioned anything that needed fixing. I've tried to carry that order of things into how I treat the people I now mentor, and I fail at it more often than you ever did, but I keep trying because of you.

So much of what I do now traces back to a habit, a phrase, or a standard I picked up from watching you work. You made me more careful, more honest about what I didn't know, and a good deal braver about the questions worth chasing. That's a rare gift, and I don't take it for granted.

Thank you for your time, your candor, and your faith in me before I'd earned it. I hope you can see some of yourself in the work I go on to do.

With gratitude and real affection,
David
